explanation: The t_learner_ridge + x_learner + r_learner ensemble with CI-based weights achieves the highest holdout lower CI (0.8949) across all 108 combinations evaluated. This represents a 0.74 percentage point improvement over the best single model (t_learner_ridge at 0.8875), which is substantial for uplift modeling. The combination provides strong model diversity: R-learner directly models treatment effects, X-learner uses imputation and is designed for heterogeneous effects, while T-learner Ridge uses separate regularized models per treatment group. The CI-based weighting (near-equal at ~33% each) suggests all three models contribute meaningfully without one dominating. The ensemble outperforms the best 2-model combination (t_learner_ridge + x_learner at 0.8928) by 0.21 points, indicating the third model adds value rather than noise.
alternatives_considered: Single t_learner_ridge (0.8875): Strong baseline but ensemble provides +0.74 points improvement
t_learner_ridge + x_learner pair (0.8928): Good performance but adding r_learner gains additional +0.21 points
Equal weights variant (0.8929): Performed 0.20 points worse than CI weights, suggesting benefit from slight optimization
Other 3-model combinations: All scored lower, validating this specific model selection
overfitting_assessment: Low overfitting risk. The selected models show reasonable CV-holdout alignment: t_learner_ridge CV lower CI was 0.9015 vs holdout contribution in ensemble of ~0.8949, r_learner CV was 0.9041, x_learner CV was 0.8837. The ensemble holdout performance (0.8949) sits conservatively below individual CV estimates, suggesting robust generalization. The near-equal weighting (~33% each) indicates stability rather than over-optimization to validation data. The fact that CI-based weights only marginally outperform equal weights (+0.002) further supports that the combination is fundamentally sound rather than overfit to a specific weighting scheme.
